metadata.summary: Structured log of threats and opportunities, each scored by probability x impact, assigned response strategy and owner with observable triggers. Every Accept requires contingency.

content.title: Risk Register
content.summary: A structured log of identified threats and opportunities, each scored by probability x impact (1-25 scale), assigned a response strategy (Avoid/Transfer/Mitigate/Accept for threats; Exploit/Share/Enhance/Accept for opportunities), and owned by a named individual. Every Accept entry requires a contingency plan or amount. The register is a living artefact: reviewed weekly, updated on new signals, closed when risks pass or materialise.

why.p: Unmanaged risks derail projects through surprise: scramble mode erodes stakeholder confidence and recovery is reactive. A scored register converts vague worry into trackable work items, forces explicit ownership, and surfaces near-certain high-impact risks before they hit. Risk-driven contingency (sum of probability x cost-exposure per open risk) beats flat 15% reserves because it calibrates to actual exposure.

Probability and impact are notoriously biased; LLMs anchor on the user's framing and produce overconfident scores. A 5x5 matrix gives a false sense of precision — risk score 12 vs 15 is mostly noise, but it drives prioritisation. "Accept" is over-used by both humans and agents to clear the register without doing real work. Static registers go stale fast; the methodology ages well only with weekly review. Positive risks (opportunities) are routinely dropped; agents focus on threats by default. Triggers and leading indicators are vague; agents need concrete sensors.

list.item: Standing up the uncertainty domain at project kickoff
Weekly risk review cycles (diffing register against issue tracker and schedule)
Pre-gate and steering-committee snapshots (top-N risks with response status)
Vendor/supplier onboarding (category=External, contract clauses as triggers)
Any milestone where "what could kill this?" deserves a written answer
Pure-Scrum teams already tracking risks as backlog impediments — duplicate tracking splits attention
Tasks under 1 week with a single owner — a standup question suffices
Pre-discovery R&D where uncertainty is the work — use a learning log instead
Risks already covered by a separate risk system (GDPR, security) — avoid reinventing

section.name: identification
section.p: Brainstorm threats and opportunities across four categories: Technical (technology failures, complexity), External (vendor delays, regulation), Organizational (resource loss, priority shifts), Project Management (poor estimates, scope creep). Techniques: team brainstorming, expert interviews, checklist review, historical project analysis.

list.item: Very Low — rare, less than 10%
Low — unlikely, 10-30%
Medium — possible, 30-50%
High — likely, 50-70%
Very High — almost certain, greater than 70%
Very Low — less than 5% budget or schedule effect
Low — 5-10% effect
Medium — 10-20% effect
High — 20-40% effect
Very High — greater than 40% or project failure

section.name: scoring
section.p: Risk Score = Probability level (1-5) x Impact level (1-5). Priority: 15-25 = High (immediate action); 8-14 = Medium (monitor closely); 1-7 = Low (periodic review).

list.item: Avoid — eliminate the threat by changing approach or removing the risky element
Transfer — shift to a third party via insurance, outsourcing, or contractual clauses
Mitigate — reduce probability or impact (prototype first, add testing, cross-train)
Accept — acknowledge and plan contingency; requires contingency_amount or contingency_plan
Exploit — ensure the opportunity occurs (assign best resources, accelerate)
Share — partner with another party to capture the benefit
Enhance — increase probability or impact (more marketing, better timing)
Accept — take the benefit if it occurs; no special action required

section.name: ownership-and-monitoring
section.p: Every risk has exactly one named owner responsible for: monitoring triggers, executing the response if the trigger fires, and updating status. Review weekly: add new risks, update P/I as situation changes, close resolved risks, track response effectiveness. Top-5 risks appear at every steering committee.

rule.statement: Every risk must have exactly one named individual as owner; "the team" is not valid.
rule.rationale: Risks without a single owner are observed, not managed. Accountability requires a name.

rule.statement: Every trigger must be an observable signal — a metric, log event, or date — not a subjective phrase like "if it gets bad".
rule.rationale: Unobservable triggers cannot be monitored; risks activate without warning.

rule.statement: Every Accept strategy entry must include a contingency_amount or contingency_plan field; Accept without contingency is invalid.
rule.rationale: Accept without a contingency is denial dressed as a strategy. When the risk materialises there is no prepared response.

rule.statement: Risk score must equal probability_level x impact_level; stored score must match computed value or the register is invalid.
rule.rationale: Score integrity is required for heatmap accuracy and prioritisation correctness.

rule.statement: Include at least one opportunity (positive risk) for every ten threats; reject registers that contain only threats.
rule.rationale: Sponsor approval rates rise when upside is made visible. LLMs drop opportunities by default.

rule.statement: Calibrate the impact scale with a project-specific reference point (e.g. "High impact = greater than $50k or greater than 1 month delay") before scoring.
rule.rationale: Uncalibrated scales produce inconsistent scores across assessors and over time.

rule.statement: Risk-driven contingency (sum of probability x cost_impact per open risk) must replace flat-percentage reserves for any project with an active risk register.
rule.rationale: Flat reserves systematically over- or under-fund based on risk profile; risk-driven calculation calibrates to actual exposure.

antipattern.description: Identifying risks once at kickoff and never updating the register.
antipattern.reason: Static registers go stale within weeks; new risks appear continuously and scored risks change as context evolves.

antipattern.description: Using "Communication breakdown" as a risk in every register.
antipattern.reason: Generic risks from LLM defaults are not project-specific; they add noise and dilute the register's signal.

antipattern.description: Scoring more than 70% of risks as Medium/Medium (3/3).
antipattern.reason: Uniform scores indicate anchoring bias; force a roughly normal distribution across the 5x5 matrix.

example.title: Five scored threats with response strategies
example.note: Each has a concrete trigger and named owner role.
example.code: | ID | Risk | Cat | P | I | Score | Strategy | Response | Owner | |------|-----------------------------|-------|---|---|-------|----------|----------------------------------|--------| | R-01 | Key developer leaves | Org | M | H | 12 | Mitigate | Document code, cross-train | PM | | R-02 | Third-party API deprecated | Ext | L | VH| 10 | Accept | Abstraction layer; monitor feeds | ArchLd | | R-03 | Scope creep from sponsor | PM | H | M | 12 | Avoid | Change control process enforced | PM | | R-04 | Performance under load | Tech | M | M | 9 | Mitigate | Load testing at sprint 3 | QA Ld | | R-05 | Budget cut mid-project | Org | L | H | 8 | Accept | Identify optional features list | PM |

example.title: Positive risks with Exploit/Enhance strategies
example.note: Often omitted by default — include at least 1 per 10 threats.
example.code: | ID | Opportunity | P | I | Score | Strategy | Response | |------|-------------------------|---|---|-------|----------|-----------------------------| | O-01 | Early delivery possible | M | H | 12 | Enhance | Fast-track critical path | | O-02 | Partnership offer | L | VH| 10 | Exploit | Prioritise integration work | | O-03 | Positive press coverage | M | M | 9 | Share | Prepare press kit in advance|

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Two-phase. Phase 1: a risk-identifier reads the project context (charter, WBS, stakeholder register, recent incidents) and brainstorms candidate risks in each PMBoK category, with explicit triggers and owners. Phase 2: a risk-scorer assigns probability × impact with rationale and proposes a strategy + response plan. Human review between phases. Weekly the agent diffs telemetry against the register and emits a CHANGES file (new risks, escalations, closures).

Recommended subagents: risk-identifier (define inline) — input: project artefacts; output: candidate risks YAML. risk-scorer (define inline) — input: candidate risks; output: scored risks with strategy + response. risk-monitor (define inline) — input: live register + telemetry feeds; output: CHANGES.md with deltas. faion-brainstorm — for the initial threat/opportunity list when context is thin. faion-sdd-executor — once a mitigation becomes a code task, drives implementation through quality gates.

example.title: Structured output schema for risk-scorer
example.note: Enforces constraints LLMs violate by default.
example.code: { "id": "R-XX", "title": "...", "category": "Technical|External|Organizational|PM", "probability": "VL|L|M|H|VH", "impact": "VL|L|M|H|VH", "score": 12, "strategy": "Avoid|Transfer|Mitigate|Accept|Exploit|Share|Enhance", "response_plan": "imperative sentence", "trigger": "concrete observable signal", "owner": "name or UNASSIGNED", "is_opportunity": false, "contingency_plan": "required if strategy=Accept", "rationale": "2 sentences max" }

list.item: Every risk has exactly one named owner; never "the team".
Triggers must be observable (metric, log event, date); reject "if it gets bad".
Calibrate P/I scales with at least one historical reference: e.g. "H impact = > $50k or > 1 month delay".
Risk-driven contingency: sum (P × I × cost) for open risks; beats flat 15% reserve.
Weekly review is a ritual, not optional; missed weeks → register goes stale.
Track top-5 risks at every steering committee; if the same five appear month after month, the responses are not working.
Capture opportunities (positive risks) — sponsor approval rates rise when you show upside.
For agile teams, surface high-score risks into the sprint backlog as risk-mitigation stories.
LLMs love brainstorming risks but score them all "Medium / Medium" — force a normal-ish distribution and reject outputs where greater than 70% are M/M.
"Communication breakdown" appears in 90% of LLM-generated registers — treat as a smell and replace with project-specific risks.
Agents drop opportunities; require a positive-risk quota.
Triggers default to "if it happens" — strip and require concrete signals.
Currency / units missing on impact ("major" with no $ or days); force a quantitative_impact field.
"Accept" gets used as a junk drawer; require contingency on every Accept.
LLMs invent risk-management ISO codes; verify any cited standard.
For very large registers (greater than 50 risks), context windows truncate — chunk by category and stitch.

template.purpose: Register table (ID, description, category, P, I, score, strategy, response, owner, status)
Single risk deep-dive card with trigger, contingency, and ownership fields

script.purpose: Validate risk YAML: score integrity, Accept+contingency rule, heatmap output
